summary: The post argues that AI makes it dangerously tempting to sprint toward new features, grand rebuilds, and ever-larger products, but that path often yields software that feels hollow rather than magical. Real “magic” comes from the unglamorous grind of care, polish, and persistence—work that still requires human attention even when AI can generate code quickly.
money_quotes: It is tempting to open Claude Code—the most popular talked-about app on today’s internet; the new Cursor; the must-have stocking stuffer of this holiday season—and YOLO-mode an expansive new feature into your product.
If everyone can build their own made-to-measure apps—decentralized apps; personal apps; custom-designed, one-of-a-kind bespoke apps—there is no market for small conveniences or narrow delights.
“Magic is just someone spending more time on something than anyone else might reasonably expect.”
AI gives software developers—and anyone creating things, for that matter—a relentless workforce that is uncannily good at tearing through new frontiers.
Because for something to be better than people believe it could be, we still have to spend more time on it than anyone would reasonably expect—even, often, ourselves.
key_insight: AI can accelerate creation, but truly great software still requires sustained human care and time well beyond what feels reasonable.
